source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Progressive Overload
content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of training stress over time, and it is the core driver of strength and muscle gains. It can be applied by adding weight, adding reps, adding sets, improving range of motion, or reducing rest. Without a steady increase in demand, the body has no reason to adapt, and progress stalls.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Double Progression
content: Double progression is a simple, reliable scheme: pick a rep range, and at a fixed load work to add reps each session until you hit the top of the range across all sets. Only then do you increase the weight and drop back to the bottom of the range. This progresses load and reps in turn and avoids jumping weight too soon.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Rep Ranges by Goal
content: Rep ranges bias the training adaptation. Roughly 3 to 6 reps with heavier loads emphasizes maximal strength; 8 to 12 reps emphasizes hypertrophy (muscle size); and 15 to 20 reps with lighter loads builds muscular endurance. All ranges build some strength and size when taken close to failure, but matching the range to the goal is more efficient.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Training Frequency
content: For building strength, training each major movement or muscle group two to three times per week generally outperforms training it once. Spreading weekly volume across more sessions keeps quality high and lets you accumulate more productive sets. Two to four total strength sessions per week suits most people balancing training with the rest of life.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Warm-Up Structure
content: An effective warm-up raises core temperature with a few minutes of easy cardio, then moves through dynamic mobility for the joints you are about to load, and finishes with progressively heavier ramp-up sets of the first exercise. This prepares the nervous system and tissues, improves performance, and lowers injury risk without causing fatigue.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Mobility and Longevity
content: Mobility — usable strength through a full range of motion — protects the joints and preserves independence with age. Tight hips, ankles, and the thoracic spine are common limiters of squatting, hinging, and overhead work. A few minutes of targeted mobility drills most days maintains range of motion and supports pain-free training for decades.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Recovery Between Sessions
content: Strength is built during recovery, not during the session itself. A muscle group typically needs 48 hours before it is trained hard again. Sleep, adequate protein, and managing total weekly stress determine how much training you can absorb. Persistent soreness, stalled lifts, and poor sleep are signs to reduce volume or intensity.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Deload Weeks
content: A deload is a planned week of reduced training stress — lighter loads, fewer sets, or both — taken roughly every four to eight weeks or when fatigue accumulates. It lets the body fully recover and supercompensate, often producing a jump in performance afterward. Deloads prevent the slow grind of overreaching that otherwise stalls long-term progress.

source: Centenarian Coach Workout Notes — Zone 2 Cardio for Longevity
content: Zone 2 cardio is steady aerobic work at an intensity where you can still hold a conversation. It builds mitochondrial density and aerobic base, which underpin cardiovascular health and healthy aging. A common target is 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week, complementing — not replacing — strength training in a longevity-focused program.
